From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Status of certain populated places in Ukraine

[![View of Khust from a hill](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/%D0%A5%D1%83%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0.jpg/250px-%D0%A5%D1%83%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A5%D1%83%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%B1%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0.jpg)

[Khust](/wiki/Khust "Khust"), one of the first Ukrainian cities to receive [Magdeburg rights](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights")

[City status](/wiki/City_status "City status") is granted by the [Verkhovna Rada](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada "Verkhovna Rada") (the national parliament of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")) to certain [populated places](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine "Populated places in Ukraine"). It is not automatically given according to any particular criteria, although since 2024 settlements with more than 10,000 people and a high population density have been eligible for city status under a specific law. In addition, the status is typically granted to settlements of historical or regional importance, even if they do not meet the population threshold.

The status was not strictly defined until the [late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"), when [Magdeburg rights](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights") spread throughout Europe, giving several settlements legally protected privileges. Under the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") in the 16th century, a system of cities and towns developed, with some cities recognized as [royal](/wiki/Royal_city_in_Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Royal city in Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") and given special rights. During this time period, as well as under the [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") empires from the 18th to the 20th centuries, city status was granted by kings and emperors. The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") was the first to establish a clearly defined system, categorizing cities by population and importance, and giving the government of [Soviet Ukraine](/wiki/Soviet_Ukraine "Soviet Ukraine") the authority to assign or change the status. Since [Ukraine's independence](/wiki/Ukraine%27s_independence "Ukraine's independence") in 1991, a similar system has been used, although the categorization of cities into those of [regional](/wiki/City_of_regional_significance_(Ukraine) "City of regional significance (Ukraine)") and [district significance](/wiki/City_of_district_significance_(Ukraine) "City of district significance (Ukraine)") was abolished in 2020.

Legally, cities do not differ significantly from other populated places ([rural settlements](/wiki/Rural_settlement_(Ukraine) "Rural settlement (Ukraine)") and [villages](/wiki/Village_(Ukraine) "Village (Ukraine)")), although city status is often seen as prestigious, helping mobilize resources and attract investors and tourists.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) A city may host the administration of a [hromada](/wiki/Hromada "Hromada") ([municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality")), in which case the hromada is classified as urban and its government is called a [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council"). A local council is in charge of [municipal services](/wiki/Municipal_services "Municipal services"), [local development](/wiki/Local_development "Local development"), and other local functions, and its administration is overseen by an [executive committee](/wiki/Executive_committee "Executive committee") headed by an elected [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor"). Cities may also be divided into subdivisions called [urban districts](/wiki/Urban_districts_of_Ukraine "Urban districts of Ukraine"), a system currently used in 24 cities. Cities with fewer than 50,000 inhabitants are classified as small and fall under a national program for their development.

As of November 2025, there are [463 cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Ukraine "List of cities in Ukraine") in Ukraine.[[4]](#cite_note-UKR_2022_estimates-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6) The largest city is [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), the country's [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city"), with a population of over 2.9 million, while the smallest inhabited city is [Uhniv](/wiki/Uhniv "Uhniv"), with an estimated 939 residents as of 2022. One city – [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat") – is fully abandoned as a result of the 1986 [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") but has not lost its city status.[[4]](#cite_note-UKR_2022_estimates-4) Kyiv and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol") are also recognized as [cities with special status](/wiki/Cities_with_special_status "Cities with special status"), meaning that they function as [first-level administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine#First_level "Administrative divisions of Ukraine") independent from the [oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine") (regions) or [autonomous republics](/wiki/Autonomous_republic_of_Ukraine "Autonomous republic of Ukraine").[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-7) [Olyka](/wiki/Olyka "Olyka") most recently received city status, in April 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-20th century")]

The [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") coast was [colonized](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation") by the [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") since the 8th century BC.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Many of the colonies later came under [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") control, including [Chersonesus](/wiki/Chersonesus "Chersonesus"), which was granted [eleutheria](/wiki/Eleutheria "Eleutheria") – the status of a free city with the right to self-government and to manage its own land and laws – by the [emperor](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor") in the first century. In contrast, [Tyras](/wiki/Tyras "Tyras") was granted [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), which likewise provided some rights, albeit limited and exercised under the supervision of a [provincial governor](/wiki/Roman_governor "Roman governor").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") under the [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"), the terms *[grad](/wiki/Grad_(toponymy) "Grad (toponymy)")* and *[gorod](/wiki/Gorod_(toponymy) "Gorod (toponymy)")* were used to refer to [fortified settlements](/wiki/Gord_(archaeology) "Gord (archaeology)"). The exact difference between the two is unclear: while historian [Pyotr Tretyakov](/w/index.php?title=Pyotr_Tretyakov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pyotr Tretyakov (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A2%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C%D1%8F%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2,_%D0%9F%D1%91%D1%82%D1%80_%D0%9D%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87 "ru:Третьяков, Пётр Николаевич")] theorized that *grad*s were fortified estates inhabited by the nobility, some of which evolved into trading centers called *gorod*s, later historian [Petro Tolochko](/wiki/Petro_Tolochko "Petro Tolochko") concluded that neither term may have carried any social meaning. From the 9th to the early 12th century, about a hundred *grad*s were mentioned in the [Rus' chronicles](/wiki/Rus%27_chronicles "Rus' chronicles"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) although the majority of Rus' settlements were rural and often unfortified, and called *ves*, *selo*, *[pogost](/wiki/Pogost "Pogost")*, or *[sloboda](/wiki/Sloboda "Sloboda")*. Some *grad*s also served as capitals of [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality"), with the major ones located on the territory of modern Ukraine being [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), [Chernigov](/wiki/Chernihiv "Chernihiv"), [Pereyaslavl](/wiki/Pereiaslav "Pereiaslav"), [Galich](/wiki/Halych#Old_Halych_(Princely_Halych) "Halych"), and [Volodimer](/wiki/Volodymyr,_Ukraine "Volodymyr, Ukraine").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The system of [Magdeburg rights](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights") developed in Europe since the 14th century, granting selected settlements special privileges including self-government, tax and judicial immunity, the right to own land, trade benefits, and exemption from most [feudal duties](/wiki/Feudal_duties "Feudal duties");[[13]](#cite_note-:8-13) however, these rights largely applied to [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") men.[[14]](#cite_note-Huban-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) They eventually replaced an existing system of [customary law](/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law") that had been in place prior to the [Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Mongol invasion of Kievan Rus'"), although the two often coexisted in individual cities.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1301%E2%80%931526) "Kingdom of Hungary (1301–1526)") was the first to grant Magdeburg rights to cities located in modern-day Ukraine, the first being [Khust](/wiki/Khust "Khust"), [Tiachiv](/wiki/Tiachiv "Tiachiv"), and [Vyshkovo](/wiki/Vyshkovo "Vyshkovo") in 1329. [Yuri II Boleslav](/wiki/Yuri_II_Boleslav "Yuri II Boleslav"), the king of [Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia"), granted these rights to [Sanok](/wiki/Sanok "Sanok") in 1339, which brings about the possibility that other major cities of the kingdom, such as [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") and Volodymyr, could have also received the same rights in that period. After the kingdom was [conquered](/wiki/Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia_Wars "Galicia–Volhynia Wars") by [Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), the system was implemented widely in the area.[[13]](#cite_note-:8-13) Kings granted Magdeburg rights to large cities such as [Lwów](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") (1356) and [Kiew](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") (1494).[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Magdeburg rights were also sometimes granted by [princely houses](/wiki/Princely_houses_of_Poland_and_Lithuania "Princely houses of Poland and Lithuania"), for example to [Lokhvytsia](/wiki/Lokhvytsia "Lokhvytsia"), [Lubny](/wiki/Lubny "Lubny"), [Pryluky](/wiki/Pryluky "Pryluky"), and [Pyriatyn](/wiki/Pyriatyn "Pyriatyn") by the [Wiśniowiecki family](/wiki/Wi%C5%9Bniowiecki_family "Wiśniowiecki family").[[13]](#cite_note-:8-13) Under the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), a system of cities ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *miasta*) and towns (*[miasteczka](/wiki/Miasteczko "Miasteczko")*) was first established, with the former status given to settlements of great importance, receiving particular rights,[[19]](#cite_note-:12-19) and the latter to settlements privately owned by the nobility that had the permission to organize markets and fairs.[[20]](#cite_note-YPS-20) Some cities were categorized as [private](/wiki/Private_town "Private town"), belonging to noble families or the church.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-19) After the issue of the [Free Royal Cities Act](/wiki/Free_Royal_Cities_Act "Free Royal Cities Act") in 1791, a special category called [royal cities](/wiki/Royal_city_in_Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Royal city in Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") was established, granting select cities special privileges including representation in the [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm_of_the_Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Sejm of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), the right to own [landed property](/wiki/Landed_property "Landed property"), security guarantees, and a possibility of [ennoblement](/wiki/Ennoblement "Ennoblement").[[21]](#cite_note-21) After the [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") was established as the result of the [Khmelnytsky Uprising](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_Uprising "Khmelnytsky Uprising"), numerous towns and cities served as [regiment](/wiki/Regiment_(administrative_unit) "Regiment (administrative unit)") centers, transforming them into important administrative, judicial, economic, and military hubs.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Cities and towns were then classified either as magisterial (those holding Magdeburg rights) or as ratusha towns, which were each headed by a chieftain known as [horodovyi otaman](/w/index.php?title=Horodovyi_otaman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Horodovyi otaman (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%93%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BD "uk:Городовий отаман")] and were subject to the jurisdiction of the general Cossack authorities.[[14]](#cite_note-Huban-14)

The last wave of the granting of Magdeburg rights occurred when the rights were granted by the [hetmans](/wiki/Hetman_of_Zaporizhian_Cossacks "Hetman of Zaporizhian Cossacks") of the Hetmanate after its [union](/wiki/Pereiaslav_Agreement "Pereiaslav Agreement") with the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), among the last being [Poltava](/wiki/Poltava "Poltava") and [Novhorod-Siverskyi](/wiki/Novhorod-Siverskyi "Novhorod-Siverskyi") by [Kyrylo Rozumovsky](/wiki/Kyrylo_Rozumovsky "Kyrylo Rozumovsky").[[13]](#cite_note-:8-13) A system of cities ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): города, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *goroda*) and towns (местечка, *[mestechka](/wiki/Mestechko_(disambiguation) "Mestechko (disambiguation)")*) continued to exist under Russia after the [liquidation of the Hetmanate](/wiki/Liquidation_of_the_autonomy_of_the_Cossack_Hetmanate "Liquidation of the autonomy of the Cossack Hetmanate").[[20]](#cite_note-YPS-20) From the early 18th century, city status was given directly by the [emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Russia "Emperor of Russia"). The 1785 [Charter for the Rights and Benefits of the Cities of the Russian Empire](/w/index.php?title=Charter_for_the_Rights_and_Benefits_of_the_Cities_of_the_Russian_Empire&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charter for the Rights and Benefits of the Cities of the Russian Empire (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%93%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%B8_%D0%B2%D1%8B%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B4%D1%8B_%D0%B3%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BC_%D0%A0%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%B9%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B9_%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%BF%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B8 "ru:Грамота на права и выгоды городам Российской империи")] issued by [Catherine II](/wiki/Catherine_II "Catherine II") defined the legal rights of city inhabitants and granted cities self-governance, establishing institutions such as [city dumas](/wiki/City_duma "City duma"), and the permission to organize markets and fairs. A city was also given a [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") and a [city plan](/wiki/City_plan "City plan") approved by the emperor. During this period, cities were categorized as capital, [governorate](/wiki/Governorate_(Russia) "Governorate (Russia)") (provincial centers), [uezd](/wiki/Uezd "Uezd") (county centers), or [non-uezd](/wiki/Supernumerary_town "Supernumerary town"). While the exact number of cities in the Russian Empire changed significantly with every administrative reform, their rights and the process of acquiring the city status remained the same.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Since the 19th century, a status called *[gradonachalstvo](/wiki/Gradonachalstvo "Gradonachalstvo")* was assigned to certain [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities "Municipalities") (for example to [Odessa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") in 1803[[24]](#cite_note-24)), positioning a city's administration under a city governor (*gradonachalnik*) subordinated directly to the [governor-general](/wiki/Governorate-General_(Russian_Empire) "Governorate-General (Russian Empire)").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

According to a decree by [Emperor Joseph II](/wiki/Emperor_Joseph_II "Emperor Joseph II") on 13 May 1784, cities of [Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Galicia and Lodomeria") – then under [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") rule – were classified into three categories. [Lemberg](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), as the regional capital, was the only first-class city; cities granted privileges by the emperor, such as additional pay for city citizenship, were categorized as royal; and urban settlements with their own [magistrates](/wiki/Magistrates "Magistrates") were categorized as municipal. In 1811, cities were defined as populated places with city privileges, while towns were granted only the right to hold fairs.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### 20th century onward

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century onward")]

Initially, the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") retained the Russian Empire's status system, additionally defining cities as urban populated places with more than 10,000 inhabitants and granting the [All-Ukrainian Central Executive Committee](/wiki/All-Ukrainian_Central_Executive_Committee "All-Ukrainian Central Executive Committee") the authority to change the status of settlements within [Soviet Ukraine](/wiki/Soviet_Ukraine "Soviet Ukraine").[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 1925, towns were abolished and replaced with [urban-type settlements](/wiki/Urban-type_settlement "Urban-type settlement"),[[28]](#cite_note-28) although some minor historic cities (such as [Chudniv](/wiki/Chudniv "Chudniv") and [Olyka](/wiki/Olyka "Olyka")) were also classified as urban-type settlements.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-:2-30) In 1930, the [Central Executive Committee of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Central_Executive_Committee_of_the_Soviet_Union "Central Executive Committee of the Soviet Union") specified that cities with fewer than 50,000 inhabitants were subordinated to the [raion](/wiki/Raion#Raions_in_the_Soviet_Union "Raion") (district) executive committees, while those with over 50,000 served as separate administrative units subordinated to the presidiums of the [republics](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union")' executive committees or to [oblast](/wiki/Oblasts_of_the_Soviet_Union "Oblasts of the Soviet Union") (regional) executive committees.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Workers' settlements were introduced in 1956 as urban populated places with at least 500 residents, most of whom worked at a single enterprise,[[32]](#cite_note-:1956-32) but this category was abolished in 1965.[[33]](#cite_note-:1965-33) Briefly, from 4 September to 30 December 1956, the oblast executive committees were in charge of city status changes.[[14]](#cite_note-Huban-14)

From 30 December 1956 onward, the authority to change the status was given to the [Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Presidium_of_the_Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic"), and cities were divided into three categories: of republican, regional, and district significance. [Cities of republican significance](/wiki/City_of_republic_significance_(disambiguation) "City of republic significance (disambiguation)") functioned as first-level administrative divisions not belonging to any oblast (region) or [Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Autonomous_Soviet_Socialist_Republics "Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republics"). They generally had a population of at least one million (at least 500,000 before 12 March 1981) and served as major economic, cultural, and administrative centers.[[32]](#cite_note-:1956-32)[[34]](#cite_note-Procedure-34) [Kiev](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol") were the only Soviet Ukrainian cities with this status, although the latter did not meet the defined population threshold.[[14]](#cite_note-Huban-14) [Cities of regional significance](/wiki/City_of_regional_significance_(Ukraine) "City of regional significance (Ukraine)") functioned as second-level administrative divisions not belonging to any raion (district), thus forming [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") that sometimes also included nearby villages. A city of regional significance was required to have a population of at least 50,000 and to possess developed housing and communal services or industry, or to have historic, recreational, or defensive importance, or the potential to develop any of these. All other cities were categorized as [cities of district significance](/wiki/City_of_district_significance_(Ukraine) "City of district significance (Ukraine)"), which generally had a population of at least 10,000 and possessed industrial enterprises, a developed economy, state housing stock, or a network of socio-cultural institutions, or the potential to develop any of these.[[32]](#cite_note-:1956-32)[[34]](#cite_note-Procedure-34)[[35]](#cite_note-:7-35)

Until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), parts of what is now Ukraine belonged to other states ([Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") and [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") to [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), [Eastern Galicia](/wiki/Eastern_Galicia "Eastern Galicia") and [Volhynia](/wiki/Volhynia "Volhynia") to [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), and [Transcarpathia](/wiki/Transcarpathia "Transcarpathia") to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic")), with city status determined by respective governments under their national laws. From the 1940s onward, the Soviet administrative system already in place elsewhere in Ukraine was extended to these territories.[[14]](#cite_note-Huban-14)

Since the [independence of Ukraine](/wiki/Independence_of_Ukraine "Independence of Ukraine") on 24 August 1991, the country has inherited the Soviet system, with all changes in city status done by the [Verkhovna Rada](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada "Verkhovna Rada") (national parliament) instead. Cities of republican significance were renamed to [cities with special status](/wiki/Cities_with_special_status "Cities with special status") with the adoption of the [Constitution of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") in 1996, which categorizes Kyiv and Sevastopol as such.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-7) The general population threshold for cities of regional significance was lowered to 30,000.[[35]](#cite_note-:7-35) Cities of regional and district significance were *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* abolished in 2020 as part of an administrative reform[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) and removed from law in 2023 along with urban-type settlements, which were reclassified as [rural settlements](/wiki/Rural_settlement_(Ukraine) "Rural settlement (Ukraine)").[[38]](#cite_note-:0-38) Currently, all [populated places in Ukraine](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine "Populated places in Ukraine") are divided into three categories (cities, rural settlements, and [villages](/wiki/Village_(Ukraine) "Village (Ukraine)")), a system established by the Constitution.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-7)

## Current legislation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Current legislation")]

### Changes in status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Changes in status")]

Article 85 of the [Constitution of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") gives the [Verkhovna Rada](/wiki/Verkhovna_Rada "Verkhovna Rada") the power to classify settlements as cities and to establish or change city boundaries.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-7) According to the law "On the Procedure for Resolving Certain Issues of the Administrative and Territorial Structure of Ukraine", [populated places](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine "Populated places in Ukraine") with a population of more than 10,000 people and a predominantly compact residential area can be classified as cities (*mista*, sg. *misto*). The law also defines two categories of settlements with largely farmstead-type residential areas: [rural settlements](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine#Rural_settlements "Populated places in Ukraine") (*selyshcha*, sg. *selyshche*), which have a population of at least 5,000, and [villages](/wiki/Village_(Ukraine) "Village (Ukraine)") (*sela*, sg. *selo*), which have a population of under 5,000. Cities are considered urban populated places, while the other two are rural. The law also provides a concrete procedure for obtaining the city status through a decision by the Verkhovna Rada, following a proposal submitted by the [Cabinet of Ministers](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") on the basis of an appeal by the local government after a [public discussion](/wiki/Public_discussion "Public discussion").[[39]](#cite_note-:3-39)[[40]](#cite_note-:1-40) As of November 2025, [Slobozhanske](/wiki/Slobozhanske,_Chuhuiv_Raion,_Kharkiv_Oblast "Slobozhanske, Chuhuiv Raion, Kharkiv Oblast") in [Kharkiv Oblast](/wiki/Kharkiv_Oblast "Kharkiv Oblast") is the only settlement to have received city status through this procedure,[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5)[[41]](#cite_note-41) while similar draft resolutions are also registered for [Borodianka](/wiki/Borodianka "Borodianka") and [Krasnoilsk](/wiki/Krasnoilsk "Krasnoilsk") on the Verkhovna Rada's website.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) Another draft resolution for [Slobozhanske](/wiki/Slobozhanske,_Dnipropetrovsk_Oblast "Slobozhanske, Dnipropetrovsk Oblast") in [Dnipropetrovsk Oblast](/wiki/Dnipropetrovsk_Oblast "Dnipropetrovsk Oblast") was withdrawn after concerns were raised that the city status would lead to higher water and transport taxes and require the creation of a separate [public utility](/wiki/Public_utility "Public utility") system,[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) although [national law](/wiki/Law_of_Ukraine "Law of Ukraine") provides no link between these potential consequences and the granting of city status.[[a]](#cite_note-47)[[46]](#cite_note-Transp-46)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Since [Ukraine's independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine") on 24 August 1991 but before the aforementioned law came into effect on 26 January 2024, 24 settlements have received city status largely on the basis of their historic, cultural, social, or economic importance rather than population: [Zlatopil](/wiki/Zlatopil,_Kharkiv_Oblast "Zlatopil, Kharkiv Oblast") (1991),[[48]](#cite_note-49) [Svitlodarsk](/wiki/Svitlodarsk "Svitlodarsk") (1992),[[49]](#cite_note-50) [Hlyniany](/wiki/Hlyniany "Hlyniany"), [Zelenodolsk](/wiki/Zelenodolsk,_Ukraine "Zelenodolsk, Ukraine"), [Pivdenne](/wiki/Pivdenne,_Odesa_Oblast "Pivdenne, Odesa Oblast"), [Novodnistrovsk](/wiki/Novodnistrovsk "Novodnistrovsk"), [Burshtyn](/wiki/Burshtyn "Burshtyn") (1993),[[50]](#cite_note-51) [Rzhyshchiv](/wiki/Rzhyshchiv "Rzhyshchiv") (1995),[[51]](#cite_note-52) [Morshyn](/wiki/Morshyn "Morshyn") (1996),[[52]](#cite_note-53) [Teplodar](/wiki/Teplodar "Teplodar") (1997),[[53]](#cite_note-54) [Shumsk](/wiki/Shumsk "Shumsk") (1999),[[54]](#cite_note-55) [Pereshchepyne](/wiki/Pereshchepyne "Pereshchepyne"), [Berezne](/wiki/Berezne "Berezne") (2000),[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) [Lanivtsi](/wiki/Lanivtsi "Lanivtsi"), [Baranivka](/wiki/Baranivka,_Zhytomyr_Oblast "Baranivka, Zhytomyr Oblast"), [Lypovets](/wiki/Lypovets "Lypovets") (2001),[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) [Olevsk](/wiki/Olevsk "Olevsk"), [Mykolaivka](/wiki/Mykolaivka,_Donetsk_Oblast "Mykolaivka, Donetsk Oblast") (2003),[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) [Perechyn](/wiki/Perechyn "Perechyn") (2004),[[62]](#cite_note-63) [Novyi Kalyniv](/wiki/Novyi_Kalyniv "Novyi Kalyniv") (2005),[[63]](#cite_note-64) [Bucha](/wiki/Bucha,_Ukraine "Bucha, Ukraine") (2007),[[64]](#cite_note-65) [Baturyn](/wiki/Baturyn "Baturyn") (2008),[[65]](#cite_note-66) [Chudniv](/wiki/Chudniv "Chudniv") (2012),[[66]](#cite_note-67) and [Reshetylivka](/wiki/Reshetylivka "Reshetylivka") (2017).[[67]](#cite_note-68) While this process remains possible, as seen in [Olyka](/wiki/Olyka "Olyka") becoming a city in April 2025 despite not meeting the population requirements,[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)[[30]](#cite_note-:2-30) it is not clearly defined in law, and the ultimate decision in such cases rests with the Verkhovna Rada. As such, special exceptions to the usual procedures may be made, as in the 2022 draft resolution proposing to grant city status to [Hostomel](/wiki/Hostomel "Hostomel") solely in recognition of its [honorary title](/wiki/Honorary_title "Honorary title") *[Hero City of Ukraine](/wiki/Hero_City_of_Ukraine "Hero City of Ukraine")*, although the proposal was withdrawn three years later without explanation.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) Similarly, the Verkhovna Rada has the power to revoke city status, through either the change of city boundaries – which has only happened once, when the city of [Inhulets](/w/index.php?title=Inhulets,_Kryvyi_Rih&action=edit&redlink=1 "Inhulets, Kryvyi Rih (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%86%D0%BD%D0%B3%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8C_(%D0%9A%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B3) "uk:Інгулець (Кривий Ріг)")] merged into [Kryvyi Rih](/wiki/Kryvyi_Rih "Kryvyi Rih") in 2002[[70]](#cite_note-71) – or the reclassification of the city as a rural settlement or a village. The Cabinet of Ministers may also abolish populated places following an appeal by a regional or local government, given that they have been officially fully abandoned for at least three years.[[39]](#cite_note-:3-39)

Cities with fewer than 50,000 inhabitants are classified as small cities.[[71]](#cite_note-:5-72) As of 2017, residents of small cities make up 22% of Ukraine's urban population.[[72]](#cite_note-:6-73) Such cities often face economic or demographic decline, climate change impacts, dependence on local government, and a lack of strategic planning and communication.[[73]](#cite_note-74) To address these challenges, the National Program for the Development of Small Cities was created in 2004. This codified legislative agenda created by the government aims to stimulate business and industry by fostering economic growth, create job opportunities, develop communication networks, and restore natural areas within small cities, among other goals. The program is financed through the state budget controlled by the Cabinet of Ministers as well as taxes collected by the city councils that are generated by economic growth caused by the program.[[71]](#cite_note-:5-72) Although the program identified a number of problems faced by small cities and demonstrated the government's awareness of their importance, it failed to meet its developmental goals by 2009 and was criticized for not addressing the issues efficiently and for duplicating the content of existing legislative acts.[[72]](#cite_note-:6-73)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

### Administration and governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_status_in_Ukraine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Administration and governance")]

Numerous cities host the administrations of [hromadas](/wiki/Hromada "Hromada") (communities), the country's third-level administrative divisions. The administrations may also be located in rural settlements or villages; however, when they are hosted by cities, they are referred to as [city councils](/wiki/City_councils "City councils"), and the corresponding hromadas are classified as urban.[[39]](#cite_note-:3-39) Each city council's administration is overseen by an [executive committee](/wiki/Executive_committee "Executive committee") headed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor"), who is elected by the hromada's residents for a five-year term. While the same procedure applies to most settlement and village councils, rural hromadas (whose administrations are located in villages) with fewer than 500 residents may opt out of establishing an executive committee. City, settlement, and village councils are in charge of a wide range of areas, including municipal property management, socio-economic development of the community, housing and [municipal services](/wiki/Municipal_services "Municipal services"), construction, transport, communications, education, healthcare, sports, and local environmental issues.[[75]](#cite_note-:4-76)

A city may also be divided into [urban districts](/wiki/Urban_districts_of_Ukraine "Urban districts of Ukraine"), which can be established and modified by the city council. The districts are managed directly by the city council, or by separate district councils that can be created by the city council, which also defines their powers and scope. An urban district council elects its own head who oversees its administration.[[7]](#cite_note-Constitution-7)[[75]](#cite_note-:4-76) Urban districts may be abolished by the city council following a public discussion.[[39]](#cite_note-:3-39) There are 108 urban districts in total across 24 cities: ten in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv");[[76]](#cite_note-77) nine each in [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk") and [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv");[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) eight in [Dnipro](/wiki/Dnipro "Dnipro");[[79]](#cite_note-80) seven in [Kryvyi Rih](/wiki/Kryvyi_Rih "Kryvyi Rih") and [Zaporizhzhia](/wiki/Zaporizhzhia "Zaporizhzhia");[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) six in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv");[[82]](#cite_note-83) five in [Makiivka](/wiki/Makiivka "Makiivka");[[83]](#cite_note-84) four in [Luhansk](/wiki/Luhansk "Luhansk"), [Mariupol](/wiki/Mariupol "Mariupol"), [Mykolaiv](/wiki/Mykolaiv "Mykolaiv"), [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa"), and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol");[[84]](#cite_note-85) three in [Horlivka](/wiki/Horlivka "Horlivka"), [Kamianske](/wiki/Kamianske "Kamianske"), [Poltava](/wiki/Poltava "Poltava"), [Simferopol](/wiki/Simferopol "Simferopol"), and [Kherson](/wiki/Kherson "Kherson");[[85]](#cite_note-86) and two in [Cherkasy](/wiki/Cherkasy "Cherkasy"), [Chernihiv](/wiki/Chernihiv "Chernihiv"), [Kropyvnytskyi](/wiki/Kropyvnytskyi "Kropyvnytskyi"), [Kremenchuk](/wiki/Kremenchuk "Kremenchuk"), [Sumy](/wiki/Sumy "Sumy"), and [Zhytomyr](/wiki/Zhytomyr "Zhytomyr").[[86]](#cite_note-87) [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi") and [Vinnytsia](/wiki/Vinnytsia "Vinnytsia") each formerly had three urban districts, but they were abolished in 2016 and 2012, respectively,[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89) while Sumy is also planning to abolish its districts.[[89]](#cite_note-:9-90) Just like other populated places and [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Administrative divisions of Ukraine"), each city and urban district is assigned a unique code under the [national register](/wiki/Codifier_of_administrative-territorial_units_and_territories_of_territorial_communities "Codifier of administrative-territorial units and territories of territorial communities") managed by the [Ministry of Communities and Territories Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Communities_and_Territories_Development "Ministry of Communities and Territories Development").[[90]](#cite_note-91)

Since [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_Metro "Kyiv Metro") and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol") are [cities with special status](/wiki/Cities_with_special_status "Cities with special status") and function as [first-level administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine#First_level "Administrative divisions of Ukraine"), they host their own [executive branches](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") separate from the city councils ([Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv_City_State_Administration "Kyiv City State Administration") and [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol_City_State_Administration "Sevastopol City State Administration") city state administrations), which are equivalent to the regional ([oblast](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine")) state administrations. The two cities' urban districts also host urban district state administrations, which operate as counterparts to the district ([raion](/wiki/Raions_of_Ukraine "Raions of Ukraine")) state administrations. These entities, collectively called local state administrations, are each headed by a [head](/wiki/Head_of_local_state_administration "Head of local state administration") who is appointed by the [president](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine") on the submission of the [Cabinet of Ministers](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") for the head of the state's term of office.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) In 2015, due to the [War in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas "War in Donbas"), local governments close to the frontline were reorganized into [civil–military administrations](/wiki/Civil%E2%80%93military_administrations_(Ukraine) "Civil–military administrations (Ukraine)"), which were formed of [military personnel](/wiki/Military_personnel "Military personnel") and headed by leaders that were appointed and dismissed by the [National Security and Defense Council of Ukraine](/wiki/National_Security_and_Defense_Council_of_Ukraine "National Security and Defense Council of Ukraine").[[93]](#cite_note-94) Similarly, since the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") on 24 February 2022, all local state administrations, as well as many city councils, have been replaced with military administrations, which broadens their power and allows the replacement of [civil servants](/wiki/Civil_servants "Civil servants") by military personnel while [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Ukraine "Martial law in Ukraine") is in effect.[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97)

A special case concerns the [abandoned cities](/wiki/Ghost_town "Ghost town") of [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl "Chernobyl") and [Pripyat](/wiki/Pripyat "Pripyat"),[[b]](#cite_note-99) which are located within the [Chernobyl exclusion zone](/wiki/Chernobyl_exclusion_zone "Chernobyl exclusion zone") and are *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* part of [Kyiv Oblast](/wiki/Kyiv_Oblast "Kyiv Oblast"), but are *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* managed separately by the [State Agency of Ukraine for Exclusion Zone Management](/w/index.php?title=State_Agency_of_Ukraine_for_Exclusion_Zone_Management&action=edit&redlink=1 "State Agency of Ukraine for Exclusion Zone Management (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%94%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B5_%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B2%D0%BE_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D0%B7_%D1%83%D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D0%B7%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%87%D1%83%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F "uk:Державне агентство України з управління зоною відчуження")] subordinated to the [Ministry of Energy](/wiki/Ministry_of_Energy_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Energy (Ukraine)") (or to [Ministry of Environmental Protection and Natural Resources](/wiki/Ministry_of_Environmental_Protection_and_Natural_Resources_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Environmental Protection and Natural Resources (Ukraine)") before its dissolution in July 2025).[[98]](#cite_note-100) Unlike other [populated places in the exclusion zone](/w/index.php?title=List_of_populated_places_in_the_Chernobyl_exclusion_zone&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of populated places in the Chernobyl exclusion zone (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A1%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%81%D0%BE%D0%BA_%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%85_%D0%BF%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%96%D0%B2_%D0%97%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B8_%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%B4%D1%87%D1%83%D0%B6%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F_(%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B0) in the exclusion zone,[[101]](#cite_note-103) making its exact population unknown, but it is inhabited by about 50 *[samosely](/wiki/Samosely "Samosely")* (illegal residents, largely those who refused to evacuate following the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster")),[[102]](#cite_note-104) as well as [power plant](/wiki/Chernobyl_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant") workers, researchers, and [border guards](/wiki/State_Border_Guard_Service_of_Ukraine "State Border Guard Service of Ukraine").[[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106)
